quote: Patience and grind, delivered entirely in Yoruba — when that was still a commercial risk.
note: The breakout that booked the first festival stage and announced a different language for Nigerian street-hop.

quote: It doesn't ask for a seat at the table. It builds a new one.
note: The mission statement of a label that had just been born — infrastructure carrying his name from now on.

quote: Confidence as choreography — a street chant turned national muscle memory.
note: The clip that swept the 2014 Nigeria Music Video Awards and made Durosoke a movement, not just a single.

quote: Street testimony delivered like folklore — the song that made OLIC feel inevitable.
note: The preceding single that framed Street OT before the album arrived, and the Street King run with it.

quote: Punch after punch, never on the canvas — the Mayweather metaphor made literal.
note: Young Jonn's beat, undefeated energy: Best Street-Hop Artiste at the Headies 2015.

quote: Shaku shaku left Agege and entered the national bloodstream.
note: Shot in Bariga, built around the #WoChallenge — Wizkid called it the biggest Nigerian song of the year.

quote: Melody-first, unhurried — built for a streaming era the Street King albums never had to court.
note: The song that made the reinvention undeniable, and one of the catalog's most-streamed chapters.

quote: Reggae and Afro-pop textures where street-hop density used to live.
note: The lead single that announced a shorter, more pop-facing Olamide — still unmistakably Baddo in the pocket.

quote: Fourteen years after Omo To Shan — two established names meeting as peers.
note: The reunion that names the Legacy era: no longer an upstart borrowing a rising star's shine.
